channel.title: Debt Desk
channel.description: Daily briefing with Morning Brief-style national and Columbus news plus commercial real estate debt and multifamily market coverage. Deals, SOFR and Treasury curve context, CMBS, debt funds, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and HUD execution. Produced by Jeff Bechtel.
channel.itunes:author: Jeff Bechtel

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk Launch: National and Columbus Headlines, Then CRE Debt and Multifamily Execution
item.description: Today’s Debt Desk opens with Morning Brief-style national and Columbus headlines, then moves into the debt desk read on SOFR, Treasury curve moves, CMBS stress, debt-fund and CRE CLO momentum, and multifamily execution across agency and HUD channels.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Thursday, April 2nd, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

Let’s get into it.

In national headlines, the White House war message and market nerves are still front and center.

In his first major national address since the conflict with Iran began, President Trump told Americans the operation was on track and urged patience, but he did not offer a clear end date. Associated Press reporting said the speech left many key strategy questions unanswered while markets continued to show stress around energy and geopolitical risk.

Elsewhere in the national macro picture, rates remain a major driver of business and consumer decision-making.

At the Treasury close on April 2nd, yields were roughly three point seven nine on the two-year, three point nine four on the five-year, four point three one on the ten-year, and four point eight eight on the thirty-year. The shape of that curve continues to frame expectations for financing costs, capital allocation, and consumer activity.

Overnight funding also remains relatively steady, though still elevated versus prior cycles.

The New York Fed’s latest SOFR print for April 1st came in at three point six five, with the thirty-day average right around the same level. That helps with confidence on floating-rate assumptions versus the whipsaw conditions we have seen in prior cycles. But we are still carrying coupons that are materially higher than the debt many sponsors are rolling off from 2021 and 2022. So even with stability, refinancing pain has not gone away.

At the same time, the broader national backdrop still shows a split between pockets of liquidity and pockets of stress.

We are seeing real issuance and transaction flow in parts of the credit stack, but also visible stress in legacy pockets. That split is likely to define the rest of 2026 more than any single rate print. Capital is available, but it is selective and pricing execution risk much more aggressively than it did in the low-rate era.

Now to Columbus and Central Ohio.

A notable state court development is moving through the local policy landscape.

WOSU reported on April 1st that the Ohio Supreme Court gave Columbus a procedural win in litigation over city gun restrictions, allowing the city to appeal a preliminary injunction path. The court did not rule on the core constitutionality question yet, but the decision keeps the legal fight active and relevant for city policy.

Housing supply and builder strategy also remain active local themes.

Builder Magazine reported April 2nd that Drees Homes is entering the Columbus market, calling it the company’s 12th operating market and third in Ohio. The article highlighted Columbus as an underbuilt housing market with strong job momentum and cited a twenty-two percent increase in tech jobs since 2023.

Quality-of-life and public investment remain another visible part of the regional story.

Axios Columbus, citing Bureau of Economic Analysis data, reported that outdoor recreation contributed about twenty billion dollars to Ohio’s 2024 economy, with more than one hundred fifty thousand jobs statewide, and noted that Columbus’ Great Southern Metro Park opened April 1st.

So that is your Columbus setup this morning: policy motion, housing expansion signals, and continued public investment in quality-of-life assets.

Now let’s shift into the Debt Desk core.

The first thing to understand right now is that deals are getting done, but the style of deal is different than what many teams got used to in 2021 and early 2022.

Today’s lenders are rewarding credible execution and punishing fragile business plans. The capital is there, but committees are requiring tighter assumptions, stronger sponsorship evidence, and better downside planning.

Three lanes are driving most of the action.

Lane one is refinance-and-extend with fresh equity support. This remains a major source of volume because so many maturities are still coming due at debt costs above existing notes.

Lane two is transitional bridge for multifamily and selected industrial where the business plan has clear, near-term cash flow milestones.

Lane three is securitized and structured channels for collateral that can clear increasingly disciplined buyer and rating expectations.

On the structured side, one of the most important positives this year has been the CRE CLO recovery in issuance pace. Trepp’s early-March read showed issuance around eleven point two billion dollars, materially above the same period last year. That is a meaningful sign that investor demand for transitional credit is functioning.

But let’s pair that with the other side of the tape.

Trepp’s March 31 update showed CMBS delinquency at seven point five five percent, up forty-one basis points month over month. So we have risk capital returning in some lanes while stress remains elevated in others. That is exactly the definition of a bifurcated market.

For borrowers, that means execution quality decides everything.

If you are bringing stable cash flow, defensible assumptions, and a documented operational plan, lenders are still willing to engage.

If you are bringing thin interest coverage, uncertain lease rollover, or a story that requires immediate cap-rate compression to work, expect lower leverage, wider spreads, and more structure.

Now to multifamily, because this is where many 2026 financing decisions are being made in real time.

At the agency level, FHFA’s 2026 caps remain eighty-eight billion dollars each for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, with mission requirements still central to program use. For qualified deals, agency execution continues to offer some of the strongest combinations of certainty, duration, and cost efficiency in the market.

Freddie’s small-balance channel also remains important because it keeps workforce and middle-market product connected to institutional capital pathways, not just the largest metro trophy assets.

The broader multifamily operating backdrop is still highly local.

In higher-supply metros, lenders are underwriting lease-up and concessions with much less optimism than they did two years ago. In lower-supply and more stable Midwest pockets, execution conditions are generally more constructive, especially for stabilized assets with clean trailing operations.

In other words, there is no single national multifamily underwriting story anymore. Submarket realities are dominating loan terms.

Debt funds continue to fill a critical role in this environment.

They are often the fastest option when sponsors need flexibility, speed, or structure outside bank and agency boxes. But that flexibility comes with higher cost and tighter expectations around milestones. The best outcomes we are seeing are when sponsors use debt funds as an intentional bridge to a defined takeout, not as an emergency patch.

HUD remains part of the conversation as well, especially for borrowers prioritizing long-duration certainty.

HUD execution is still slower than other channels, but in the right deal it can provide durable financing that is hard to replicate elsewhere. In a market where benchmark uncertainty remains elevated, that duration value is real.

Now let’s tie all this together into today’s practical playbook.

Quote across lanes, not just one lane. Borrowers make better decisions when they can see bridge, agency, and structured options side by side.

Underwrite to realistic occupancy and expense assumptions now, not optimistic stabilization narratives later.

Make your refinance plan explicit at closing. Do not wait until year two to decide the takeout path.

Keep a close eye on benchmark composition. The two-year, five-year, and thirty-year are all shaping decisions right now, not just the ten-year headline.

Treat volatility as a base case, not an exception case. Structure reserves and covenants accordingly.

Before we close, let me add a deeper debt-desk read on what is actually moving committees this week, because this is where deals are winning or dying.

On leverage, we continue to see lenders favoring a lower initial advance paired with future-funding flexibility, rather than stretching day-one proceeds. That approach gives sponsors capital for the plan, but only as execution proves out. In practice, that means the old conversation of “How much can I borrow today?” is being replaced by “How much can I earn as I hit milestones?” It is a healthier framework, but it requires better operating discipline.

On structure, reserves are now doing far more work than they did in the low-rate cycle. Interest reserves, TI and LC reserves, and capex escrows are not just check-the-box terms anymore. They are often the credit bridge that gets a deal from maybe to yes. If you are advising borrowers, be explicit about that early. A deal that looks expensive on coupon alone can still be the right execution if structure protects liquidity through the hardest part of the business plan.

On sponsorship, transparency is being priced in real time. Sponsors with clear monthly reporting, realistic assumptions, and an honest variance narrative are consistently getting better outcomes than sponsors who try to “sell” the pro forma. The market is far less punitive of a tough story than it is of an unclear story.

On multifamily specifically, the most common underwriting miss right now is still timing. Teams underestimate how long it takes for concessions to burn off and for stabilized operations to truly settle. If your plan requires perfect timing, the structure needs to absorb delay risk. If your structure cannot absorb delay risk, the leverage should come down.

And on takeouts, this is where many transactions quietly improve. When borrowers line up their likely refinance lane at origination, they make better day-one decisions on capex pacing, lease strategy, and reserve usage. When they do not, they often optimize for the first loan and get trapped at refinance.

So the practical advice this morning is simple: underwrite like you are already in year three of the loan, not month three. If the asset and structure still work under that lens, your odds of execution and refinance success go up materially.

Now a quick markets snapshot before we close.

SOFR most recently printed at three point six five.

Treasury curve at the April 2 close was about three point seven nine on the two-year, three point nine four on the five-year, four point three one on the ten-year, and four point eight eight on the thirty-year.

CMBS delinquency, per Trepp’s late-March read, is seven point five five percent.

CRE CLO issuance pace has improved meaningfully year over year, showing risk appetite has returned selectively.

And Columbus continues to show economic and housing momentum signals, even as policy and affordability pressures remain active local variables.

One thing to watch by tomorrow morning:

Watch whether front-end Treasury and SOFR stability holds while credit spreads stay contained. If rates stay orderly, transaction momentum can build quietly. If we get another volatility shock, committees will slow, leverage will compress, and execution windows will narrow again.

Final takeaway for this Thursday: discipline is now the market’s competitive advantage. The teams that are closing are not necessarily the teams with the most aggressive terms. They are the teams with the clearest operating plan, the cleanest data room, and the most realistic refinancing path. That is true for sponsors, originators, and asset managers alike.

If you are heading into lender conversations today, lead with credibility, not just upside. Show the downside plan, show the reserve logic, and show exactly how this deal performs if lease-up takes longer than hoped. In this market, clarity gets funded.

That is your Debt Desk for Thursday, April 2nd, 2026.

I will see you tomorrow.
